text: Thailand has always fascinated me because it's a wonderful country with imposing temples, natural scenery, and dream beaches! I was stunned by my trip to Koh Samui with the Big Buddha, the stunning beaches of Chaweng and Lamai, and the Hin Lad waterfall. I then explored the Ang Thong Marine Park, and then marveled at Bangkok, where I marveled at the beauty of various temples, particularly the Royal Palace with the Emerald Buddha and the Temple of the Reclining Buddha! Then, on a second trip, I reached the other coast, where I was enraptured by the heavenly beaches and landscapes of Phi Phi Island, Maya Bay, and Krabi! I was also enchanted by the spectacular sunsets visible from Patong Beach. I was also completely absorbed by the magical, oriental atmosphere, which, especially at the Buddhist temples I visited, such as Chalong and the Big Buddha, had allowed me to trigger a deep introspective journey that had then favored and determined the achievement of an inner peace very similar to Nirvana! Luciano

I was honestly in shock learning that women weren’t allowed in the temple shown in the video just because they menstruate. I was really looking forward to visiting Chiang Mai, but I think I’ll stay clear of any places that discriminate against women.

text: Hi Renata, you did such a good job and probably know Chiang Mai temples more than average Thai person.  And the guy who kept popping up in your background was quite entertaining and makes your clip more lively 🙈🙉🙊 Just want to share with you a few things about Buddhism.  Before Buddha statues were constructed (since they really didn't know what the Buddha looked like or perhaps no artisans could perfectly capture the Buddha image), people used elephants to represent the Buddha.  When people circumambulate around the cetiya/stupa, which the Buddha relics were usually held, they are paying respect to the Buddha like when they circumambulated his body three times when he was alive.  And the structures in front of the doors/gates of the temples are not dragons, they are naga (dragons), half human and half serpent, who protects the Triple Gems, so they are there to guard the doors/gates of the temples.  Hope to see more of your videos.

text: Hey! I love your video. I think it is nicely shot and very informative. I can see that you have learned the culture and etiquettes before coming to Thailand. However, there is a few incorrect information here. Firstly, despite the way it’s written, Thailand or Thai is pronounced with the T not Th. Secondly, the dragon you saw is actually not a dragon, it is called Phaya-nak(พญานาค). It’s like a holy snake, unlike dragon which is related to Chinese culture. Thirdly, the reason why Thai people walk around the pagoda in circle 3 times is actually not related to the elephant but the 3 jewels. There are three jewels in Buddhism: the Buddha, the teaching, and the disciples(monks).

text: The dragon is actually called a naga, a mythical water serpent that Hindus and Buddhists believe dwell in rivers and streams. Buddhist legend goes that the king of nagas once listened to a sermon of the Buddha and was so move he wanted to become a monk, but Buddha did not allow this because only humans could become monks. So in despair, the Naga king asked to be the protector of Buddhism and that before all monks are to be ordained that they be called Naga so that the merit from their action may help him one day become human and have an opportunity to enter the monk hood and follow in Buddha’s teachings as well.
video_title: THAILAND: Chiang Mai Old City - Best things to do | day and night 🌞🌛

text:
The “Dragon” or Naga is also a mythical creature who learned of Buddha’s teachings and wanted to achieve Nirvana, but Buddha said Nirvana can only be achieve as a human in this realm, and heartbroken, the naga asked to become the guardian of Buddha and Buddhism, until he can attain a human birth, and achieve nirvana and liberation from samsara.

When a person is ordained as a monk, one of the question they must answer is “are you human?”. Since a naga cannot lie, he will not be allowed to be ordained. To pay homage to the devotion of the naga, the period before a man becomes a monk, he is referred to as “naga”, and each time someone is ordained and becomes a good monk, the naga earns good karma that will him achieve Nirvana when his time is come in his human existence.

In mainland Southeast Asia, some folk beliefs still consider the naga their ancestor.

text: Most of Chiang Mai's ancient ruins are in perfect condition, having survived the destruction of the past war. When Burma will raise an army to attack Ayutthaya, it will use Chiang Mai as a place to store food supplies. When the Lan Xang Kingdom of Laos wanted to expand the kingdom, it annexed Chiang Mai in its power. There was only one time Chiang Mai was almost destroyed when King Chai Rachathirat of Ayutthaya raised an army to defeat Chiang Mai, who had fallen under the influence of Lan Xang. Chiang Mai then agreed to swamipak to Ayutthaya. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MOSMmm3FAHQ
